items.stem: Tracy is reviewing the three building blocks of Claude Cowork Automation. Which definition correctly describes a Claude plugin?

options.text: A saved, reusable instruction set that tells Claude what format to produce and what constraints to apply — equivalent to a written SOP Tracy would follow manually.
options.rationale: Wrong because this definition describes a Claude skill, not a plugin. Skills encode instructions and output formats; plugins handle connections to external systems.

options.text: A connector that gives Claude read or write access to an external business system such as Jira or Confluence, handling authentication and data formatting so skills can retrieve live data.
options.rationale: Correct because a plugin is the integration layer between Claude and an external system. It handles authentication, querying, and data formatting, while skills call plugins to obtain live information.

options.text: A Claude configuration that autonomously plans and executes a multi-step sequence — calling skills and invoking other connectors — until it reaches a stopping condition or human-in-the-loop checkpoint.
options.rationale: Wrong because this definition describes an agent, which orchestrates skills and plugins across multiple steps. A plugin only provides the connection to one external system.

options.text: A subscription-based access tier that Tracy purchases to unlock third-party integrations across her entire Advisor360° team.
options.rationale: Wrong because this conflates commercial licensing concepts with the technical definition. A plugin is a technical connector, not a licensing product; it requires explicit authorization but is not a subscription tier.

items.stem: Tracy uses the RSTRM framework to evaluate automation candidates. What do the five letters of RSTRM stand for?

options.text: Repeatable, Scalable, Testable, Reliable, Measurable
options.rationale: Wrong because these are plausible-sounding automation quality attributes but are not the RSTRM criteria. Confusing quality attributes with suitability criteria is a common surface-pattern error.

options.text: Repetitive, Standardized, Time-sensitive, Resource-intensive, Modular
options.rationale: Wrong because several terms share the same first letter as the real criteria but refer to different concepts. 'Standardized' conflates with 'Structured,' and 'Time-sensitive' inverts the meaning of 'Time-consuming.'

options.text: Reliable, Scalable, Transferable, Repeatable, Measurable
options.rationale: Wrong because these are plausible-sounding software quality attributes but are not the RSTRM automation-suitability criteria. Confusing quality attributes with suitability criteria is a common surface-pattern error.

options.text: Repetitive, Structured, Time-consuming, Rules-based, Multi-step
options.rationale: Correct because RSTRM stands for Repetitive, Structured, Time-consuming, Rules-based, Multi-step — the five criteria Tracy applies to evaluate whether a workflow is a strong candidate for Claude Cowork automation.

items.stem: Tracy reads this claim from a colleague: 'Any task Tracy performs more than once a week is a strong automation candidate for Claude Cowork, regardless of whether it follows consistent rules.' Is this claim true or false? Provide a one-sentence justification citing the RSTRM framework.
items.expected_answer: False. Frequency alone (satisfying only the Repetitive criterion) is insufficient — a task must also be Structured, Time-consuming, Rules-based, and Multi-step to rate as a high-confidence automation candidate; a frequently repeated task that requires subjective judgment fails the Rules-based criterion and is not reliably automatable.

items.stem: Tracy explains to a new team member at Advisor360° how skills and plugins differ. Which of the following correctly captures the relationship between them?

options.text: Skills and plugins are interchangeable names for the same component; the choice of term depends only on whether the workflow is triggered manually or automatically.
options.rationale: Wrong because skills and plugins are distinct components with separate responsibilities. Skills encode the instruction logic; plugins provide the external-system connection. They are not interchangeable.

options.text: A plugin is a subordinate part of a skill; every skill bundles its own plugin code so that no separate plugin authorization is required.
options.rationale: Wrong because this encodes the misconception that skills connect directly to external systems without a separate plugin. In practice, skills call plugins for live data; plugins require independent authorization and can be revoked separately.

options.text: A skill encodes what to do with data (instructions, format, constraints), while a plugin provides access to an external system (authentication, querying, data formatting); a skill calls a plugin to retrieve live data from Jira or Confluence.
options.rationale: Correct because skills and plugins have complementary but distinct roles: the skill is the 'what and how' layer, and the plugin is the 'connection and retrieval' layer. Tracy's status-report skill calls the Jira and Confluence plugins to fetch live ticket and page data.

options.text: Plugins execute the full automation end-to-end, while skills exist only to give users a simplified interface for triggering those plugins.
options.rationale: Wrong because this inverts the relationship. Skills contain the logic and formatting instructions; plugins are the connectors. A plugin does not execute the full automation — an agent does that when multiple steps are required.

items.stem: Tracy is evaluating a second workflow: every Friday afternoon she reviews all ServiceNow incident tickets opened that week, assigns a severity bucket (P1/P2/P3) based on impact and urgency fields already filled in by the reporting team, and pastes the bucketed list into a Confluence page. The task takes 45 minutes per week. Applying the RSTRM framework, which verdict is best supported?

options.text: Weak automation candidate — the task is Repetitive and Time-consuming but fails Rules-based because severity bucketing requires professional judgment that Claude cannot replicate.
options.rationale: Wrong because the severity rules are explicitly encoded in the impact and urgency fields already present in ServiceNow. When rules are articulable and consistent, the task passes the Rules-based criterion. The misconception here is that any classification task requires subjective judgment.

options.text: Strong automation candidate — the task is Repetitive (weekly, predictable), Structured (ServiceNow fields are standardized), Time-consuming (45 min/week × 50 weeks = 37.5 hours/year), Rules-based (severity tiers map directly to impact/urgency fields), and Multi-step (retrieve, classify, write to Confluence).
options.rationale: Correct because the task satisfies all five RSTRM criteria: consistent weekly cadence (Repetitive), standardized ServiceNow input and Confluence output (Structured), material time cost (Time-consuming), explicit field-based severity rules (Rules-based), and three discrete steps — retrieve, classify, publish (Multi-step).

options.text: Partial automation candidate — the task passes RSTRM but should not be automated because Claude cannot write to Confluence.
options.rationale: Wrong because a Confluence plugin provides Claude with write access to Confluence pages. The claim that Claude cannot write to Confluence reflects a pre-course assumption that this chapter's plugin concept directly corrects.

options.text: Weak automation candidate — the task is not Multi-step because it involves only a single output (the bucketed Confluence page).
options.rationale: Wrong because Multi-step refers to the number of discrete process steps, not the number of output artifacts. Retrieve from ServiceNow, classify tickets, write to Confluence is a three-step sequence, which meets the Multi-step criterion.

items.stem: Tracy wants to pull the ten most recently closed Jira tickets from her current sprint into a bullet list — no synthesis, no multi-system retrieval, just a formatted list from one source. Which Claude building block is minimally sufficient for this task, and why?

options.text: A plugin alone — because a plugin connects to Jira and returns the ticket data; no skill or agent is needed for a single-source retrieval with no transformation logic.
options.rationale: Wrong because a plugin provides the connection to Jira but does not define the output format or constraints. Tracy still needs a skill to specify what data to request, which fields to include, and what list format to produce.

options.text: An agent — because any workflow that produces a deliverable (the bullet list) requires autonomous multi-step orchestration.
options.rationale: Wrong because this encodes the misconception that an agent is needed for any multi-step task including single-source retrievals. A single retrieval plus formatting is a skill-plus-plugin operation; an agent adds unnecessary complexity.

options.text: A skill alone (no plugin required) — because read-only Jira access is embedded in Claude's training data and does not require a live connector.
options.rationale: Wrong because this encodes the misconception that skills connect directly to external systems without a plugin. Claude's training data does not include Tracy's live Jira board; a Jira plugin is always required to retrieve current ticket data.

options.text: A skill calling the Jira plugin — because the task is a single-step retrieval with a defined output format, which matches the skill-plus-plugin pattern; no agent-level orchestration across multiple steps or systems is required.
options.rationale: Correct because pulling from one system and formatting the output is exactly the skill-plus-plugin use case. The skill specifies the instruction (which tickets, which fields, bullet-list format); the plugin handles the Jira connection and authentication. An agent would be over-engineered for a single retrieval.

items.stem: During the worked example, Tracy evaluates her weekly status report workflow using the RSTRM framework and rates the Structured criterion. She notices that her Slack project channel contains free-text messages that do not follow a machine-readable format. Which conclusion does the RSTRM framework support for the Structured criterion in this case?

options.text: The workflow passes the Structured criterion because the RSTRM definition of 'Structured' means consistently extractable — Slack messages follow recognizable patterns for decisions and blockers — and Tracy's human-in-the-loop checkpoint handles ambiguous cases that pattern-based extraction flags for her review.
options.rationale: Correct because the RSTRM framework's Structured criterion requires consistent extractability, not a rigid machine format. Slack messages at Advisor360° follow consistent patterns; Claude performs first-pass classification and Tracy validates edge cases. This is the explicit design decision (DP-2) from the worked example.

options.text: The workflow fails the Structured criterion because Slack messages are free-text and therefore unstructured — the workflow should be redesigned to require team members to post in a fixed template before automation is attempted.
options.rationale: Wrong because 'Structured' in the RSTRM framework means consistently extractable, not machine-formatted. Slack messages follow consistent patterns for decisions and blockers even in free-text form. Requiring a rigid template is an unnecessary constraint that the framework does not mandate.

options.text: The workflow passes the Structured criterion provisionally, but only after Tracy manually converts all past Slack messages into a structured CSV export before each automation run.
options.rationale: Wrong because the RSTRM framework does not require pre-processing inputs into a different format before each run — that would eliminate the time-saving benefit of automation. Structured means the patterns are consistent enough for Claude to extract; Tracy's review at the human-in-the-loop checkpoint handles residual ambiguity without a manual pre-conversion step.

options.text: The workflow fails the Structured criterion because any free-text input source causes automatic failure, and Tracy should replace Slack with a structured ticketing system before attempting automation.
options.rationale: Wrong because this encodes the misconception that Claude can only process machine-formatted inputs. The RSTRM framework defines Structured as 'consistently extractable,' not 'machine-formatted.' Consistent natural-language patterns satisfy the criterion.

items.stem: Tracy's colleague argues: 'Once the status report automation has run successfully for three months, the human-in-the-loop checkpoint before distribution can be safely removed — the automation has proven itself reliable.' Is this argument sound? Provide a one-sentence justification that identifies the flaw in the reasoning.
items.expected_answer: False. Past reliability does not eliminate the risk of a future error that would reach leadership unreviewed; the human-in-the-loop checkpoint exists to protect Tracy's professional credibility on each individual distribution — an error on run 97 is just as damaging as an error on run 1, so the checkpoint is a permanent design element, not a temporary safeguard.

items.stem: Tracy's new intern writes the following description of how the weekly status report automation works: 'The agent reads Jira tickets and formats them into a bullet list. A plugin then calls the skill to synthesize drafts from Confluence and Slack. The skill sends the final report to leadership after Tracy approves.' Identify all the role-assignment errors in this description and state what each component actually does.
items.expected_answer: Three errors: (1) 'The agent reads Jira tickets' — reading Jira is a plugin function (the Jira plugin retrieves ticket data); the agent orchestrates the sequence but does not itself read from Jira. (2) 'A plugin then calls the skill' — the relationship is inverted; a skill calls a plugin to retrieve data, not the other way around. (3) 'The skill sends the final report to leadership' — sending is an output action that requires a plugin (e.g., a Gmail or Slack plugin); a skill encodes instructions and formatting but does not independently deliver to external destinations.

items.stem: Tracy is presenting the RSTRM framework to her Advisor360° program management team as a screening tool for identifying automation candidates. A team member challenges her: 'The RSTRM framework is too demanding — if a task has to pass all five criteria strongly, almost nothing will qualify.' Tracy disagrees. In two to three sentences, construct Tracy's rebuttal, referencing at least one specific rating outcome from the RSTRM framework that justifies automating tasks that do not score Strong on every criterion.
items.expected_answer: Tracy should argue that the RSTRM framework does not require all five criteria to be rated Strong — tasks with a mix of Strong and Partial ratings can still be viable automation candidates, with appropriate design compensations. For example, a task that scores Partial on the Structured criterion (such as Slack message classification) can be handled by having Claude perform a first-pass extraction that Tracy validates at a human-in-the-loop checkpoint, rather than abandoning automation entirely. The framework is a calibration tool, not a binary gate: two or more Weak ratings signal a redesign, but one Partial rating with a mitigation strategy is an acceptable design decision.
